fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

fr:title.text: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,\approx _{\mathcal {A}}, \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}\right \rparen \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {}\left \lparen \approx _{\mathcal {A}}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}} : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x of reflexivity data to each vertex.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph. A displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B}\equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet },\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet }\right \rparen \mathclose {} over \mathcal {A} is given by families of types \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {}\left \lparen \approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\bullet }\right \rparen \mathclose {} : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lbrace x,y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rbrace \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lbrace x,y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rbrace \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lbrace x,y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rbrace \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lbrace x,y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rbrace \mathclose {} }} x \approx _{\mathcal {A}} y\to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B}}^{x}{u} : b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}}b for every u:Bx.

Given a displayed reflexive graph \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A} and a vertex x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} , we shall write \mathcal {B} \mathopen {}\left \lbrack x\right \rbrack \mathclose {} for the component of \mathcal {B} at x:A, which we define to be the following reflexive graph:

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

jms-indent:row.fr:tex: \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,u\right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,v\right \rparen \mathclose {} :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x\approx _{\mathcal {A}}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x\approx _{\mathcal {A}}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x\approx _{\mathcal {A}}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x\approx _{\mathcal {A}}y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} u\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p}v

fr:title.text: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The discrete reflexive graph with vertices in a type A, written \mathord {\vartriangle }A, is defined by setting x\approx _{\mathord {\vartriangle }A} y :\equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen x = y\right \rparen \mathclose {} and \mathsf {rx}_{\mathord {\vartriangle }A}{x} :\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let B\colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \to A creates a reflexive graph structure \mathopen {}\left \lbrace x:\mathcal {A}\,\middle \vert \,Bx\right \rbrace \mathclose {} on \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx as follows:
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} be a family of reflexive graphs indexed in a type A. We define the product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B} x of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

jms-indent:row.fr:tex: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {} :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {}

f \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B} x} g :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} f x \approx _{\mathcal {B} x} g x

\mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B} x}{f} :\equiv \lambda {x}\mathpunct {.}\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {B} x} \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {}

fr:title.text: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {B} be a family of reflexive graphs indexed in a type A. We define the coproduct \mathchoice {\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B}{x} of the family \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

jms-indent:row.fr:tex: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathchoice {\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B}{x}\right \rvert \mathclose {} :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B} x\right \rvert \mathclose {}

\mathopen {}\left \lparen x,u\right \rparen \mathclose {} \approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \coprod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathcal {B}{x}} \mathopen {}\left \lparen y,v\right \rparen \mathclose {} :\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x=y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x=y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x=y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p:x=y\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} p_*^{ \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {B}{-}\right \rvert \mathclose {} } u \approx _{\mathcal {B}{y}} v

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, it suffices to show any other g :\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is contractible with centre f.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

Let A be an arbitrary type and let B\colon A\to \mathsf {Type} be a family of n-truncated types. Then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is n-truncated.

By induction on n, with the base cases following from and respectively. In the inductive step, we need to show that for all f,g\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx the identification type f=g is n+1-truncated assuming the same for the identification types of each Bx. By , f=g is a retract of \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx and the latter is n+1-truncated by our inductive hypothesis. Therefore, the result follows from .
Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via and .

Let f\colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {}\left \lparen f\circ -\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen C\to A\right \rparen \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lparen C\to B\right \rparen \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.

fr:title.text: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.

To identify two witnesses \phi ,\phi ' \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}x = y that A is a proposition, it suffices via to exhibit a double homotopy in A identifying each \phi xy with \phi 'xy. By , this follows from our assumption that A is a proposition.

Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{D\cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type}\\ D_1&:D_0\to D_0\to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_0&: D_0\to C_0\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\to C \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_0x,F_0y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible}\,F_0\\ h_2 &: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_1xy\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_1xy\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type}\\ D_1&:D_0\to D_0\to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_0&: D_0\cong C_0\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\cong C \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_0x,F_0y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

\begin {aligned} D_1&: \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\cong C \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda {x,y}\mathpunct {.}{C \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} ,\approx _{\mathcal {A}}, \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}\right \rparen \mathclose {} is given by a type \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} of vertices together with a family of types \mathopen {}\left \lparen \approx _{\mathcal {A}}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Type} of edges and an assignment \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}} : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} x of reflexivity data to each vertex.

Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
